text: The surgeon positions the robotic da Vinci SI system ports and divides the falciform ligament using ultrasonic shears up to the hepatic veins. The falciform ligament is retracted with an endo loop. The hilar dissection begins with scissors in arm one and cadier forceps in arm two. The right hepatic artery is looped, and the right portal vein is dissected and clipped using a hemoloclip applicator. The right portal vein is divided, revealing color demarcation. Parenchymal surface marking is performed, and robotic firefly technology is used to visualize the line of demarcation.

The liver is retracted using rubber band stitches on either side, providing traction for parenchymal transection. The console surgeon performs the transection using ultrasonic shears and Maryland bipolar forceps, while the assistant surgeon uses CUSA for deeper transection and suctioning. The Segment V vein draining into the middle hepatic vein is identified, clipped, and divided. Hemostasis is achieved with bipolar and ultrasonic shears. The hilar plate is dissected and divided intraparenchymally, with the robotic firefly system using ICG to verify bile duct division.

The surgeon loops and divides the large inferior right hepatic vein using an endo-GIA stapler. The segment eight vein is isolated, clipped with hemoloc and metal clips, and divided. Subsequently, the right hepatic vein is divided using an endo-GIA stapler.

The surgeon releases all lateral attachments to the right lobe of the liver, including diaphragmatic attachments to the posterior superior aspect of the tumor. Robotic instruments provide sustained traction, facilitating dissection in difficult areas. The right lobe is lifted from the retroperitoneum, and the hepatocaval ligament is divided using an endo-GIA stapler, completing the parenchymal transection.
